step.title: Step 8
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 13, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 12 of about 30 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The eyes are at different heights now:
- Left eye: rows 39-43, cols 19-23
- Right eye: rows 44-48, cols 39-43
Wait, that's strange. The left eye is at rows 39-43 but the right eye is at rows 44-48. They're at different vertical positions! This means the UP action only moved the left eye up, not both eyes. Let me reconsider the mechanics.
Actually, looking back at the earlier data:
- After the initial UP: both eyes moved to rows 44-48 (from 49-53)
- After DOWN: both eyes moved back to rows 49-53
- After LEFT: left eye cols 14-18, right eye cols 44-48 (both at rows 49-53)
- After RIGHT: both eyes back to cols 19-23 and 39-43 (both at rows 49-53)
- After 6 UP moves: left eye at rows 39-43, right eye at rows 44-48
So the 6 UP moves didn't move both eyes equally. The left eye moved up more than the right eye. This suggests that UP might move only one eye at a time, or there's some collision detection happening.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the eyes move independently and UP moves the left eye up while DOWN moves the right eye down? Or maybe the eyes move toward/away from each other?
Let me look at this more carefully. The initial position was both eyes at rows 49-53. After 1 UP, both were at rows 44-48. After 1 DOWN, both were back at rows 49-53. So UP and DOWN initially moved both eyes together.
But now after 6 more UP moves (total 7 UP moves from original), the left eye is at 39-43 and right eye is at 44-48. This means at some point the right eye stopped moving up while the left continued.
This could mean:
1. The eyes have independent movement limits
2. The right eye hit a boundary/obstacle
3. The movement mechanic changed
Looking at the face shape, the right side might have a different boundary. Let me check the face structure on the right side vs left side.
Actually, looking at the ascii crop, I see the face is not perfectly symmetrical. The left side has the mouth opening wider, while the right side narrows sooner. This could explain why the right eye stopped moving up earlier.
Let me try to understand the goal better. Maybe I need to position both eyes at the same height, or at specific positions. Let me try moving the right eye up to match the left eye, or try other combinations.
Actually wait - maybe UP/DOWN move individual eyes, not both. Let me re-examine. When I first pressed UP, both eyes moved from 49-53 to 44-48. But maybe that's because both eyes were at the same starting position.
Let me try pressing UP again to see what happens now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Eyes are at different heights now. Let me try UP and DOWN to understand mechanics better
# Current: left eye rows 39-43, right eye rows 44-48

# Try UP
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After UP:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"  Eye: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Try DOWN
result = action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After DOWN:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"  Eye: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After UP:
  Eye: rows 39-43, cols 19-23
  Eye: rows 44-48, cols 39-43
After DOWN:
  Eye: rows 44-48, cols 19-23
  Eye: rows 49-53, cols 39-43
